state.planMarkdown:
Bot: WhatsApp Token Intelligence Bot for EVM Contract Analysis

Summary:
A production-ready WhatsApp bot that lets users paste EVM token contract addresses in direct messages or group chats and receive concise token intelligence reports. The bot detects addresses, resolves the likely chain, fetches live token and market data, runs deterministic risk checks, optionally summarizes findings with AI, and returns a readable risk assessment optimized for WhatsApp.

The service is designed as the bot brain only. It runs as a single Node.js ESM process, uses CookMyBots managed transport for WhatsApp message delivery, and optionally persists user settings, group settings, watchlists, cached reports, and analysis history in MongoDB. No WhatsApp Cloud API webhooks or WhatsApp platform credentials are required inside this bot service.

Product goals:
1) Make contract analysis effortless in WhatsApp.
Users should be able to paste a contract address into a DM or group and receive a clear report without learning commands. The bot should also support explicit commands for power users.

2) Support multiple EVM chains.
The bot should analyze token contracts across major EVM-compatible networks by using chain-aware address resolution, chain-specific RPC/data providers, and chain-specific risk heuristics.

3) Provide concise, useful risk summaries.
Reports should avoid overwhelming users. Each response should include a risk level, key warnings, token identity, liquidity and trading context, contract risk signals, holder/distribution observations, and suggested next steps.

4) Work well in both DMs and groups.
In DMs, the bot can respond to every detected contract address. In groups, it should respect configurable behavior such as only responding when mentioned, only responding to commands, or automatically analyzing detected addresses.

5) Let users and groups maintain watchlists.
Users should be able to add tokens to a personal or group watchlist, view tracked tokens, remove tokens, and request refreshes. Watchlists can later support alerting for risk changes, liquidity changes, price movement, or ownership changes.

6) Build for safe production use.
The system should be rate-limited, observable, cache-heavy, and defensive against spam, repeated addresses, provider failures, malicious inputs, and AI hallucination.

High-level architecture:
The bot is a single Node.js ESM service acting as the decision engine for CookMyBots. CookMyBots handles WhatsApp connectivity, message delivery, and transport-specific concerns. The Node.js service receives normalized inbound message events from CookMyBots and sends normalized outbound replies back through the CookMyBots integration.

Core components inside the single process:

1) Message intake and normalization.
This layer receives events from CookMyBots, normalizes sender, chat, group, message text, mentions, quoted messages, timestamps, and message IDs, and passes them into the routing layer. It should keep platform-specific logic minimal so the bot remains focused on intelligence behavior.

2) Command and address router.
This layer decides whether a message is a command, a passive contract address detection event, a watchlist action, or an ignored message. It should support direct messages and group policies separately. For groups, it should check whether auto-detection is enabled, whether the bot was mentioned, and whether the sender has permission to change group settings.

3) Contract detection and chain resolution.
The bot detects EVM-style addresses using strict validation. If the user provides a chain hint such as eth, bsc, base, arbitrum, polygon, optimism, or avalanche, that chain is used first. Without a chain hint, the bot attempts resolution across configured EVM chains, prioritizing known watchlist entries, recent group context, liquidity presence, verified token metadata, and chain confidence scores. If multiple likely matches exist, the bot asks the user to choose.

4) Analysis orchestrator.
This component coordinates all data collection and scoring. It fetches token metadata, pair and liquidity information, price data, holder distribution, contract verification status where available, ownership/admin signals, tax or transfer behavior where available, known risk lists, and recent activity. It should run independent checks concurrently but enforce per-analysis timeouts so WhatsApp responses stay fast.

5) Risk engine.
The risk engine produces a deterministic score and label before AI is involved. Example labels: Low, Medium, High, Critical, Unknown. It combines contract signals, liquidity signals, market signals, holder concentration, ownership controls, blacklist or honeypot indicators where available, suspicious metadata, proxy or upgradeability concerns, and data confidence. The AI layer may summarize and explain the result, but it should not be the sole source of truth.

6) AI summarization layer.
The AI layer converts structured findings into a concise WhatsApp-friendly report. It should be constrained to the collected facts and deterministic risk output. It should not invent liquidity, holders, audit status, or safety claims. The prompt should clearly require uncertainty language when data is missing.

7) Report formatter.
This component creates short and long report formats. In groups, default to a short report with a risk label, top warnings, token basics, and commands for details. In DMs, a fuller report can be returned. Reports should include timestamps and chain names so users understand freshness and context.

8) Persistence layer.
MongoDB can store users, chats, group settings, watchlists, cached analysis results, address aliases, usage logs, and audit events. If persistence is not required for a minimal build, in-memory maps can support caching and rate limits, but production should use MongoDB for watchlists and settings.

9) Cache and rate control layer.
The bot should cache analysis results briefly by chain and address to prevent repeated expensive lookups. It should also rate-limit per user, per group, and globally within the single process. For repeated group messages containing the same address, the bot can reuse the latest report or reply with a short cached summary.

10) Provider abstraction.
External blockchain, market, contract, and AI providers should be wrapped behind interfaces so providers can be replaced without rewriting bot logic. The bot should degrade gracefully when a provider is unavailable and clearly show which parts of the report have low confidence.

Data model:
MongoDB collections can be kept simple and aligned with the bot’s core objects. If MongoDB is not enabled, equivalent in-memory maps can be used for non-critical caches, but watchlists and settings should be persisted for production.

1) users.
Stores WhatsApp user identity as provided by CookMyBots, first seen time, last seen time, preferences, default chain, personal rate-limit state if persisted, and optional flags such as admin or blocked.

2) chats.
Stores DM or group chat identity, chat type, group title if available, first seen time, last seen time, and bot behavior settings. For groups, settings should include auto-detect enabled or disabled, mention-required mode, allowed commands, admin-only configuration changes, and preferred chains.

3) watchlists.
Stores watched tokens for a user or group. Fields should include owner type, owner ID, chain ID, contract address, display symbol, display name, notes, tags, added by, created time, last analyzed time, last risk label, and optional alert preferences.

4) tokenreports.
Stores cached analysis snapshots by chain ID and contract address. Fields should include normalized token metadata, data source timestamps, liquidity summary, market summary, contract signals, holder signals, risk score, risk label, AI summary, confidence level, errors or missing-data notes, created time, and expiry time.

5) analysishistory.
Stores a lightweight history of analyses requested by users or groups. Fields should include requester ID, chat ID, chain ID, address, trigger type, report ID, response status, latency, and timestamp. This is useful for abuse detection, analytics, and debugging.

6) addressaliases.
Optionally stores resolved aliases for common tokens or user-defined labels. Fields should include chain ID, address, symbol, name, aliases, source, confidence, and updated time.

7) usagelimits.
Optionally stores rolling counters if rate limits must survive restarts. In a single-process deployment, in-memory counters may be enough for basic operation, while MongoDB-backed counters are better for production durability.

Important indexing choices:

1) Unique index for watchlist owner type, owner ID, chain ID, and contract address.
2) Index for tokenreports by chain ID, contract address, and expiry time.
3) Index for analysishistory by chat ID and timestamp.
4) Index for users and chats by platform identity.
5) Index for addressaliases by chain ID, address, symbol, and aliases.

Key flows:
1) Passive address analysis in a DM.
A user pastes a contract address. The router detects a valid EVM address, checks user rate limits, resolves the chain, checks the cache, and starts analysis if needed. The bot replies with a concise report containing risk label, token basics, chain, liquidity summary, top warnings, confidence level, and follow-up commands such as watch, full, or refresh.

2) Passive address analysis in a group.
A group member posts a contract address. The router checks group settings. If auto-detect is disabled or mention-required mode is enabled and the bot was not mentioned, the message is ignored. If allowed, the bot performs the analysis and posts a short report to avoid group spam. If the same address was recently analyzed in the group, the bot can reply with a cached summary and the timestamp.

3) Explicit analyze command.
A user sends a command such as analyze eth 0x... or scan 0x.... The bot treats this as an intentional request even in groups, subject to rate limits and permissions. If chain is omitted and multiple chains match, the bot asks the user to choose from likely options.

4) Multi-chain ambiguity flow.
If the same address appears on several chains with meaningful token data, the bot returns a short selection prompt. The user can respond with a number or chain name. The bot stores the pending selection briefly in memory or MongoDB and continues the analysis after the user chooses.

5) Watchlist add flow.
A user or group member sends watch eth 0x.... The bot resolves and analyzes the token, then stores it in the personal or group watchlist depending on context and command variant. The bot confirms with token symbol, chain, current risk label, and how to view the watchlist.

6) Watchlist view flow.
A user sends watchlist. In DMs, the bot returns the personal watchlist. In groups, it returns the group watchlist unless the user explicitly asks for personal. The list should be compact, showing symbol, chain, latest risk label, last checked time, and address suffix.

7) Refresh flow.
A user sends refresh eth 0x... or refresh watchlist. The bot bypasses normal cache where appropriate, fetches fresh data, updates the stored report and watchlist last risk label, then returns changes since the last report if available.

8) Full report flow.
After a short group result, a user can request full eth 0x... or full report. The bot can send a longer response in the group if allowed, or suggest DM for detailed output. The full report includes deeper contract, liquidity, holder, and confidence details.

9) AI risk summary flow.
The deterministic risk engine creates structured findings first. The AI layer receives only those findings and generates a plain-language summary. The final response includes factual risk drivers and uncertainty notes. If the AI provider fails, the bot still returns the deterministic report.

10) Failure and degradation flow.
If market data is unavailable, the bot still reports contract metadata and states that liquidity data could not be verified. If chain RPC fails, it asks the user to retry later. If an address is invalid or not a contract, it explains the issue clearly. If a user exceeds rate limits, it returns a short cooldown message.

Tech stack:
The service should be implemented as a single Node.js application using ESM. It is the bot brain only: message logic, command handling, analysis orchestration, persistence, formatting, rate control, and integration with CookMyBots managed transport.

WhatsApp transport is handled by CookMyBots. The bot must not implement WhatsApp Cloud API webhooks and must not require WHATSAPPTOKEN, WHATSAPPPHONENUMBERID, or WHATSAPPVERIFYTOKEN. The service should only use the CookMyBots integration contract available to the project for receiving normalized messages and sending replies.

Persistence should use MongoDB, which is already integrated, for production storage of users, chats, settings, watchlists, cached reports, and analysis history. Simple in-memory maps are acceptable for short-lived pending selections, short TTL caches, and local rate-limit counters when durability is not required.

The blockchain data layer should use configurable EVM RPC and data-provider integrations through internal provider abstractions. The design should support multiple EVM chains by chain ID, chain name, explorer metadata where available, RPC endpoint configuration, native currency metadata, and enabled or disabled status.

The AI layer should use a configurable AI text generation provider through an abstraction. AI should only summarize structured findings generated by the deterministic analysis engine and should not replace factual checks.

File or image storage is not required for the initial version. If later reports include generated images or exported files, use object storage or simple filesystem storage generically, without coupling the architecture to a specific vendor.

Deployment should be a generic Node.js server in the cloud. The service runs as one process and should be configured with environment variables for CookMyBots integration details, MongoDB connection details if enabled, chain provider settings, AI provider settings, feature flags, and rate-limit thresholds. Do not add separate databases, message queues, cache servers, or additional infrastructure.

Non-functional requirements:
1) Reliability.
The bot should respond even when some providers fail. Analysis should be assembled from partial data when necessary, with clear confidence notes. External calls should have timeouts, retries where safe, and circuit-breaker style disabling inside the process when a provider is repeatedly failing.

2) Latency.
Most reports should return within a WhatsApp-friendly response window. Use concurrent provider calls, short cache TTLs, and progressive detail levels. A short initial report is preferable to a delayed exhaustive report.

3) Rate limiting and abuse protection.
Apply limits per user, per group, and globally. Groups need stricter controls to prevent spam. Repeated identical addresses should use cached reports. Invalid address spam should trigger cooldowns.

4) Security.
Validate all user input. Never execute user-provided content. Store only necessary WhatsApp identifiers and bot settings. Protect provider credentials through environment configuration. Avoid logging sensitive payloads unnecessarily.

5) Accuracy and explainability.
The risk score should be deterministic and traceable to specific signals. The report should show why a token is considered risky, not just provide a label. AI-generated text should be constrained by structured facts and reviewed through formatting rules.

6) Data freshness.
Every report should include an analysis timestamp. Cached reports should be marked as cached when reused. Watchlist items should store last checked time and latest known risk status.

7) Group usability.
Group responses should be compact by default. Admins should be able to disable auto-detection, require mentions, or restrict settings changes. The bot should avoid replying to every occurrence of the same address in busy groups.

8) Observability.
Log request IDs, chat IDs in a privacy-conscious form, command names, provider latency, analysis outcomes, cache hits, and error categories. Store lightweight analysis history in MongoDB for debugging and product analytics.

9) Maintainability.
Keep chain support, provider integrations, risk rules, command definitions, and response templates modular. Adding a new EVM chain should require configuration and provider support rather than major code changes.

10) Compliance and disclaimers.
Reports should include a short non-financial-advice disclaimer where appropriate, especially in full reports. The bot should frame results as risk intelligence, not investment recommendations.

Bot commands:
• analyze — Analyze a token contract address and return a concise risk report. Works with an explicit chain or with automatic chain detection. (usage: analyze eth 0x123... or analyze 0x123...) [analysis]
• scan — Short alias for analyze. Useful in groups when users want a quick report. (usage: scan base 0x123...) [analysis]
• full — Return a more detailed report for a token, including deeper contract, liquidity, holder, and confidence notes. (usage: full eth 0x123...) [analysis]
• refresh — Fetch fresh data for a token and update the cached report. Can also refresh all watchlist entries if enabled. (usage: refresh eth 0x123... or refresh watchlist) [analysis]
• watch — Add a token to the current personal or group watchlist after resolving and analyzing it. (usage: watch eth 0x123...) [watchlist]
• unwatch — Remove a token from the current personal or group watchlist. (usage: unwatch eth 0x123...) [watchlist]
• watchlist — Show tracked tokens for the current DM or group, including chain, symbol, latest risk label, and last checked time. (usage: watchlist) [watchlist]
• settings — Show current bot settings for the chat, including auto-detect mode, mention-required mode, preferred chains, and group behavior. (usage: settings) [administration]
• autodetect — Enable or disable automatic contract address detection in the current group. This should be admin-only in groups. (usage: autodetect on or autodetect off) [administration]
• mentionmode — Require the bot to be mentioned before it analyzes pasted addresses in a group. This should be admin-only in groups. (usage: mentionmode on or mentionmode off) [administration]
• chains — List supported EVM chains and their short names for commands. (usage: chains) [help]
• help — Show available commands and examples for analyzing tokens and managing watchlists. (usage: help) [help]

planSections.productGoals:
1) Make contract analysis effortless in WhatsApp.
Users should be able to paste a contract address into a DM or group and receive a clear report without learning commands. The bot should also support explicit commands for power users.

2) Support multiple EVM chains.
The bot should analyze token contracts across major EVM-compatible networks by using chain-aware address resolution, chain-specific RPC/data providers, and chain-specific risk heuristics.

3) Provide concise, useful risk summaries.
Reports should avoid overwhelming users. Each response should include a risk level, key warnings, token identity, liquidity and trading context, contract risk signals, holder/distribution observations, and suggested next steps.

4) Work well in both DMs and groups.
In DMs, the bot can respond to every detected contract address. In groups, it should respect configurable behavior such as only responding when mentioned, only responding to commands, or automatically analyzing detected addresses.

5) Let users and groups maintain watchlists.
Users should be able to add tokens to a personal or group watchlist, view tracked tokens, remove tokens, and request refreshes. Watchlists can later support alerting for risk changes, liquidity changes, price movement, or ownership changes.

6) Build for safe production use.
The system should be rate-limited, observable, cache-heavy, and defensive against spam, repeated addresses, provider failures, malicious inputs, and AI hallucination.
planSections.highLevelArchitecture:
The bot is a single Node.js ESM service acting as the decision engine for CookMyBots. CookMyBots handles WhatsApp connectivity, message delivery, and transport-specific concerns. The Node.js service receives normalized inbound message events from CookMyBots and sends normalized outbound replies back through the CookMyBots integration.

Core components inside the single process:

1) Message intake and normalization.
This layer receives events from CookMyBots, normalizes sender, chat, group, message text, mentions, quoted messages, timestamps, and message IDs, and passes them into the routing layer. It should keep platform-specific logic minimal so the bot remains focused on intelligence behavior.

2) Command and address router.
This layer decides whether a message is a command, a passive contract address detection event, a watchlist action, or an ignored message. It should support direct messages and group policies separately. For groups, it should check whether auto-detection is enabled, whether the bot was mentioned, and whether the sender has permission to change group settings.

3) Contract detection and chain resolution.
The bot detects EVM-style addresses using strict validation. If the user provides a chain hint such as eth, bsc, base, arbitrum, polygon, optimism, or avalanche, that chain is used first. Without a chain hint, the bot attempts resolution across configured EVM chains, prioritizing known watchlist entries, recent group context, liquidity presence, verified token metadata, and chain confidence scores. If multiple likely matches exist, the bot asks the user to choose.

4) Analysis orchestrator.
This component coordinates all data collection and scoring. It fetches token metadata, pair and liquidity information, price data, holder distribution, contract verification status where available, ownership/admin signals, tax or transfer behavior where available, known risk lists, and recent activity. It should run independent checks concurrently but enforce per-analysis timeouts so WhatsApp responses stay fast.

5) Risk engine.
The risk engine produces a deterministic score and label before AI is involved. Example labels: Low, Medium, High, Critical, Unknown. It combines contract signals, liquidity signals, market signals, holder concentration, ownership controls, blacklist or honeypot indicators where available, suspicious metadata, proxy or upgradeability concerns, and data confidence. The AI layer may summarize and explain the result, but it should not be the sole source of truth.

6) AI summarization layer.
The AI layer converts structured findings into a concise WhatsApp-friendly report. It should be constrained to the collected facts and deterministic risk output. It should not invent liquidity, holders, audit status, or safety claims. The prompt should clearly require uncertainty language when data is missing.

7) Report formatter.
This component creates short and long report formats. In groups, default to a short report with a risk label, top warnings, token basics, and commands for details. In DMs, a fuller report can be returned. Reports should include timestamps and chain names so users understand freshness and context.

8) Persistence layer.
MongoDB can store users, chats, group settings, watchlists, cached analysis results, address aliases, usage logs, and audit events. If persistence is not required for a minimal build, in-memory maps can support caching and rate limits, but production should use MongoDB for watchlists and settings.

9) Cache and rate control layer.
The bot should cache analysis results briefly by chain and address to prevent repeated expensive lookups. It should also rate-limit per user, per group, and globally within the single process. For repeated group messages containing the same address, the bot can reuse the latest report or reply with a short cached summary.

10) Provider abstraction.
External blockchain, market, contract, and AI providers should be wrapped behind interfaces so providers can be replaced without rewriting bot logic. The bot should degrade gracefully when a provider is unavailable and clearly show which parts of the report have low confidence.
